planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in almost any well-drained to moist soil in full sun to full shade; mat rush is extraordinarily adaptable and among the most drought-, heat-, and salt-tolerant of all ornamental grass-like plants. Established plants require essentially no supplemental water in zone 8+ after their first summer. The cultivar 'Tanika' is compact and widely used in commercial landscapes.

directions: Plant in virtually any well-drained soil in full sun to full shade; mat rush is an Australian native sedge-like perennial that has become one of the most widely used tough ornamental plants in warm-climate landscaping worldwide — valued for an exceptional tolerance of drought, heat, poor soil, salt spray, waterlogging, shade, and neglect that makes it nearly indestructible once established. The plant produces dense, arching mounds of stiff, strap-like, dark-green leaves 2–3 feet tall and wide, with clusters of small, cream-yellow, sweetly fragrant flowers borne just above or within the foliage in late spring. It is extensively used in commercial streetscaping, highway medians, coastal plantings, and erosion-control work because of its reliability under minimal maintenance. 'Tanika' is a compact cultivar with finer, more graceful foliage that has become the dominant selection in commercial use. Outstanding for mass planting on difficult dry slopes, under trees in dry shade, or as a no-maintenance edging in zone 7–11 gardens.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or plant container-grown specimens in consistently wet to standing-water conditions in full sun to part shade; umbrella plant grows in boggy soil, shallow water up to 6 inches deep, or ordinary moist container mixes. It can be overwintered indoors as a houseplant in cold climates and is one of the most popular container and pond plants.

directions: Grow in consistently wet soil, boggy margins, or shallow standing water in full sun to part shade; umbrella plant is a Madagascar native sedge in the papyrus family producing tall, straight, triangular stems topped with graceful, radiating whorls of narrow, grass-like bracts that spread like the ribs of an umbrella — an architectural silhouette immediately distinctive in any water garden or container planting. It grows best in shallow water (2–6 inches) or in saturated boggy soil at pond margins, and is equally successful as a houseplant in a water-filled saucer. The upright, bamboo-like stalks create bold vertical interest in water features, and the plant is prolific enough to supply cut foliage for arrangements. In zones 9–11 it is perennial outdoors; in colder climates it is widely grown as a pool ornamental moved indoors for winter or maintained as a houseplant year-round. Propagation is uniquely simple — cut off a head of bracts and float it upside-down in water, where it will root from the bracts.

planting.note: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in sharply drained, sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in fall; peacock moraea grows through the cool season (October–April) and requires complete dryness during summer dormancy. In zones 9–10 with perfect drainage, corms can remain in the ground year-round. In other climates, lift corms after foliage dies and store dry until fall.

directions: Plant corms in fall in sharply drained, lean, sandy or gritty soil in full sun; peacock moraea is one of the most spectacular members of the iris family — a South African endemic whose three outer petals each bear an iridescent, peacock-eye marking of brilliant blue-purple or violet in the center, surrounded by a contrasting ring of yellow or orange against a white or purple petal background. This extraordinary pattern closely mimics the eyespot of a peacock feather, making each flower an art object of botanical precision. The plant is a winter-grower of the Western Cape fynbos and blooms in spring (February–April), producing flowers that last only a single day each but appear in succession from multiple buds. It requires the characteristic fynbos growing cycle of wet winters and bone-dry summers, making perfect drainage and summer dryness absolute requirements. Fascinating for the specialist bulb grower and extraordinary as a cut flower or container specimen in a cold frame where the summer dry period can be managed.

planting.note: Plant corms 2 inches deep in sharply drained, sandy, lean soil in full sun; spider iris is a winter-growing South African fynbos plant requiring dry summers and moist cool winters. In zones 9–10 with excellent drainage it can be grown in the open garden with a reliable summer dry period. Naturalizes freely in California coastal climates similar to its native habitat.

directions: Plant corms in fall in sharply drained, lean, sandy soil in full sun; spider iris is among the most bizarre and visually fascinating members of the iris family — a South African fynbos endemic producing flowers of extraordinary complexity, with six wavy, crinkled, spider-leg-like tepals in cream, brown, purple-black, and maroon arranged in intricate combinations and textures, the wavy margins and darkly mottled patterns creating a flower that resembles a small, ornate mosaic. The flowers are short-lived (each lasting a single day or two) but appear successively over several weeks in winter and spring and carry a distinctive, musky-vanilla fragrance that is strong and reminiscent of chocolate to some observers. It grows through the cool season of its native winter-rainfall Western Cape habitat and must be kept completely dry in summer. Related to the moraeas, it belongs to the same iridaceae tribe and requires identical cultivation. A collector's plant of unique character, grown for its botanical bizarreness as much as its fragrance.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, humus-rich, neutral to slightly acidic soil in part to full shade; New Zealand rock lily is particularly suitable for dry shade under trees where it succeeds where many competitors fail. It tolerates brief frosts to approximately 20°F but performs best in zones 8b–10 in frost-free or near-frost-free conditions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich soil in part to full shade; New Zealand rock lily is a handsome, clump-forming perennial native to New Zealand's coastal and lowland forests that produces arching rosettes of broad, strap-like, glaucous, gray-green to blue-green leaves in a loosely spreading clump, then in spring sends up branching, airy sprays of small, nodding, pure-white, star-shaped flowers with distinctive reflexed petals that hover above the foliage on slender stems — an effect of elegant, understated refinement. The 'renga' in its Maori name refers to its use as a food source (the fleshy roots were an important carbohydrate in traditional Maori diet). In gardens, it is valued as a shade-tolerant, drought-tolerant groundcover for mild climates — one of the few plants producing genuinely ornamental flowers in dry shade under large trees. The selection 'Te Puna' has especially good, compact foliage. Outstanding as a container specimen and as mass planting in shaded coastal gardens.
